expertise_area: History, biology, economics, and climate science with focus on pandemics, Roman Empire, and slavery

key_insights.insight: Plague caused massive mortality spikes shaping human population trends over the last 10,000 years.
key_insights.quote: the overwhelming, not just one, but the overwhelming two major features in human population, going back the last 10,000 years, is this one bacteria, right?

key_insights.insight: Climate change and plague together created a double shock that contributed to the Roman Empire's decline.
key_insights.quote: I think we now can say pretty clearly that environmental factors like climate, but also especially diseases play a part in that really big transformation.

key_insights.insight: Mortality during plague events reached 50-60%, far above normal death rates, causing societal shock.
key_insights.quote: the death rate might have been close to or even over 60% wherever the black death.

key_insights.insight: The Roman economy was advanced but lacked sustained innovation due to absence of science and creative destruction.
key_insights.quote: they go as far as you can with Smithian advantages to market exchange and specialization, to banks and finance, but without the kind of creative destruction of new technologies that improve productivity...

key_insights.insight: Slavery in Rome was widespread (10-20%) but not racialized, justified by property law, and lacked abolition movement due to ideology and repression.
key_insights.quote: Slavery is really a prominent institution in Rome from the late Republic... they build an economy that really relies on slave labor in important sectors of the economy.

key_insights.insight: Transition from foraging to farming increased infectious disease burden, contradicting the Eden myth.
key_insights.quote: the transition from foraging to farming... it definitely increased the infectious disease burden.

topics: Industrial revolution prerequisites (science, empiricism, useful knowledge)

actionable_advice.advice: Invest in basic science and empiricism to drive sustained innovation.
actionable_advice.how_to: Fund fundamental research and promote experimental methods through grants and institutions like royal societies.

actionable_advice.advice: Strengthen public health infrastructure (vaccination, sanitation) to mitigate disease impacts.
actionable_advice.how_to: Support vaccine development, clean water access, and hygiene programs based on lessons from historical pandemics.

actionable_advice.advice: Preserve and digitize ancient texts using modern technology to recover lost knowledge.
actionable_advice.how_to: Back projects like the Vesuvius Challenge that use AI and imaging to read damaged scrolls.

contrarian_claims.claim: The Roman Empire might have survived if not for the plague and climate shock, challenging the view of inevitable decline.
contrarian_claims.context: Most historians attribute the fall to internal decay; Harper argues external shocks were decisive and a Mediterranean core could have persisted.

contrarian_claims.claim: Slavery was not the main factor preventing an industrial revolution in Rome; lack of science and innovation was more critical.
contrarian_claims.context: Some argue slave labor discouraged mechanization; Harper disputes this, noting advanced sectors used slaves yet still lacked innovation due to absent scientific culture.
